news_summary: Recent advances highlight breakthroughs in anti-aging skin treatment, innovative automation in scientific research, new insights into vitamin D's role in pain management, and significant health improvements from substantial weight loss using Ozempic.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées récentes mettent en lumière des percées dans le domaine des traitements anti-âge pour la peau, des innovations en matière d'automatisation dans la recherche scientifique, de nouvelles découvertes sur le rôle de la vitamine D dans la gestion de la douleur, ainsi que des améliorations significatives de la santé grâce à une perte de poids importante obtenue grâce à Ozempic.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances ponen de relieve los avances en los tratamientos antienvejecimiento para la piel, la automatización innovadora en la investigación científica, los nuevos conocimientos sobre el papel de la vitamina D en el tratamiento del dolor y las mejoras significativas en la salud derivadas de una pérdida de peso considerable gracias al uso de Ozempic.

articles.title: Breakthrough drug reverses aging in skin and dramatically speeds healing
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that a topical anti-aging drug called ABT-263 can dramatically improve wound healing in older skin. The treatment works by removing damaged “senescent” cells that accumulate with age and slow the body’s repair process. In aged mice, wounds healed much faster after treatment, while the drug also activated genes tied to collagen production and tissue regeneration.
articles.content:
A drug designed to eliminate worn out, aging cells may help older skin recover from injury much faster, according to research published in Aging (Aging-US). The work, titled "Topical ABT-263 treatment reduces aged skin senescence and improves subsequent wound healing," suggests that targeting "zombie cells" in the skin could one day improve healing after surgery, injury, or chronic wounds in older adults.

The research team included Maria Shvedova, Rex Jeya Rajkumar Samdavid Thanapaul, Joy Ha, Jannat Dhillon, Grace H. Shin, Jack Crouch, Adam C. Gower, Sami Gritli, and Daniel S. Roh from Boston University Aram V. Chobanian and Edward Avedisian School of Medicine.

Clearing Out Aging Cells

As skin gets older, damaged cells can accumulate instead of dying off. These cells, known as senescent cells, no longer work normally, but they remain active enough to interfere with nearby tissue. Over time, they can release inflammatory signals and other molecules that weaken the skin's ability to repair itself.

The researchers tested whether ABT-263, a senolytic drug, could reduce this burden when applied directly to aged skin. Senolytic drugs are designed to selectively remove senescent cells, which have been linked to aging, inflammation, and slower tissue repair.

In the study, aged mice received ABT-263 on their skin for five days. After treatment, the skin showed fewer signs of cellular aging. When the researchers then created small wounds, the treated mice healed more quickly than untreated mice.

By day 24, 80% of the mice treated with ABT-263 had fully healed wounds, compared with 56% of untreated mice.

A Surprising Healing Boost

One of the more unexpected findings was that ABT-263 briefly increased inflammation in the skin. In many cases, inflammation is seen as harmful, especially when it becomes chronic. But in this case, the short burst appeared to help prepare the skin for repair.

The treatment seemed to wake up healing pathways that are normally sluggish in older tissue. Gene activity increased in areas tied to wound repair, including collagen production, blood vessel growth, tissue remodeling, and other processes needed to close and strengthen damaged skin.

This matters because aging skin does not just wrinkle or thin. It also becomes less responsive after injury. That slower response can increase the risk of prolonged recovery after surgery, delayed closure of wounds, and complications in people with chronic skin injuries.

Why Topical Treatment Matters

ABT-263 has drawn interest because it can target senescent cells, but oral senolytic drugs may cause side effects because they circulate through the body. Applying the drug directly to the skin could offer a more focused approach.

In this study, topical ABT-263 reduced signs of senescence in aged mice, but it did not appear to have the same effect in young mice. That suggests the treatment may be most active in older tissue, where senescent cells have built up.

The researchers believe this targeted approach could be especially useful before surgery or in people at risk for poor wound healing. Instead of waiting for a wound to struggle, a treatment might one day help prepare older skin in advance.

"Our study underscores the potential of topical senolytic treatments to enhance wound healing in aging skin, presenting a potentially promising strategy for preoperative care."

Newer Research Points in the Same Direction

Since this 2024 work, the broader field has continued moving toward localized senolytic strategies for skin repair. A 2025 review in Ageing Research Reviews described cellular senescence as a key contributor to skin aging and skin disease, while noting that senolytics and related therapies could become useful tools for targeting harmful senescent cells in the skin.

A 2026 study took the idea further in diabetic wound healing, a major medical challenge often marked by chronic inflammation, poor blood vessel growth, and cellular senescence. Researchers developed a localized wound dressing carrying ABT-263 and reported that it reduced senescent cell burden, improved healing in diabetic mice, and showed no detectable systemic toxicity in that model.

At the same time, scientists are careful not to portray senescent cells as purely bad. A 2024 Frontiers in Immunology review emphasized that senescence can play a helpful role during normal wound repair, but persistent senescent cells may contribute to chronic wounds, fibrosis, and abnormal healing. The challenge is timing and precision: removing the harmful lingering cells without disrupting the useful early repair signals.

Promise With Important Caution

The findings are exciting, but they are still early. The ABT-263 skin study was done in mice, and more work is needed before scientists know whether the treatment is safe or effective in people.

Researchers will also need to answer key questions about dosing, timing, long term safety, and whether the benefits apply to human skin, surgical recovery, diabetic wounds, or other slow healing conditions.

Still, the idea is powerful. By clearing away cells that hold aging skin back, topical senolytic treatments could someday help the body repair itself more quickly and more effectively. For older adults facing surgery or chronic wounds, that could make healing less difficult, less risky, and far faster.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough with a topical drug that removes senescent cells to dramatically improve wound healing in aged skin, which has broad implications for older adults' health and recovery. The study details the mechanism, experimental results, and potential future applications, demonstrating substantial positive real-world impact and meaningful scope beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New skin cream helps older skin heal wounds faster by removing aging cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As people get older, their skin becomes slower to heal after injuries because damaged cells called senescent cells build up and interfere with repair.

**What happened** – Scientists tested a drug called ABT-263 on the skin of older mice. This drug removes senescent cells when applied as a cream. After five days of treatment, the mice’s wounds healed much faster than untreated mice, with 80% fully healed by day 24 compared to 56% without the drug.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows that clearing damaged cells can wake up the skin’s natural healing processes, like making collagen and growing new blood vessels. Unlike pills that affect the whole body, this drug is applied directly to the skin, which could reduce side effects. Faster healing can help older adults recover better from surgeries or chronic wounds.

**What's next step** – Researchers need to test this treatment in humans to check if it’s safe and effective. They also want to learn the best doses and timing. Future studies might explore using the cream before surgery or for people with slow-healing wounds, including those with diabetes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new skin cream that removes aging cells shows promise in helping older skin heal wounds faster by activating natural repair processes.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle crème cutanée aide la peau des personnes âgées à guérir plus vite en éliminant les cellules vieillissantes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En vieillissant, la peau cicatrise plus lentement car des cellules endommagées appelées cellules sénescentes s’accumulent et gênent la réparation.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont testé un médicament nommé ABT-263 sur la peau de souris âgées. Ce médicament élimine les cellules sénescentes lorsqu’il est appliqué en crème. Après cinq jours de traitement, les plaies des souris guérissaient beaucoup plus vite qu’avant, avec 80 % complètement guéries au jour 24 contre 56 % sans traitement.

**Impact** – Ce résultat est important car il montre que supprimer ces cellules endommagées peut réveiller les mécanismes naturels de guérison, comme la production de collagène et la croissance de nouveaux vaisseaux sanguins. Contrairement aux comprimés qui agissent sur tout le corps, ce médicament est appliqué directement sur la peau, ce qui pourrait limiter les effets secondaires. Une cicatrisation plus rapide aide les personnes âgées à mieux récupérer après une opération ou des blessures chroniques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs doivent tester ce traitement chez l’humain pour vérifier sa sécurité et son efficacité. Ils veulent aussi déterminer les meilleures doses et le bon moment pour l’utiliser. Des études futures pourraient envisager d’appliquer la crème avant une opération ou chez des personnes avec des plaies qui guérissent lentement, comme les diabétiques.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle crème qui élimine les cellules vieillissantes montre un potentiel pour aider la peau âgée à cicatriser plus rapidement en activant ses processus naturels de réparation.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva crema para la piel ayuda a que la piel envejecida sane heridas más rápido al eliminar células viejas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que las personas envejecen, su piel tarda más en sanar porque las células dañadas llamadas células senescentes se acumulan y dificultan la reparación.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos probaron un medicamento llamado ABT-263 en la piel de ratones viejos. Este medicamento elimina las células senescentes cuando se aplica como crema. Después de cinco días de tratamiento, las heridas de los ratones sanaron mucho más rápido que sin el medicamento, con un 80 % completamente curado en 24 días comparado con un 56 % sin tratamiento.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque demuestra que eliminar estas células dañadas puede activar los procesos naturales de curación de la piel, como producir colágeno y crecer nuevos vasos sanguíneos. A diferencia de las pastillas que afectan todo el cuerpo, este medicamento se aplica directamente en la piel, lo que podría reducir los efectos secundarios. Una curación más rápida puede ayudar a los adultos mayores a recuperarse mejor después de cirugías o heridas crónicas.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores necesitan probar este tratamiento en humanos para saber si es seguro y funciona bien. También quieren estudiar la mejor dosis y momento para usarlo. En el futuro, podrían usar la crema antes de cirugías o para personas con heridas que tardan en sanar, como quienes tienen diabetes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva crema que elimina células envejecidas muestra potencial para ayudar a la piel vieja a sanar heridas más rápido al activar sus procesos naturales de reparación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an aged, weathered tree with patches of dull, withered bark being gently peeled away by glowing, translucent hands that reveal fresh, vibrant new bark underneath, alongside small glowing seeds sprouting rapidly into healthy shoots around the base, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones.

articles.title: A multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 May 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10652-yA multi-agent system for automating scientific discovery
articles.content: Scientific discovery is driven by the iterative process of observation, hypothesis generation, experimentation, and data analysis. Despite recent advancements in applying artificial intelligence to biology, no system has yet automated all these stages [1, 2, 3]. Here, we introduce Robin, the first multi-agent system capable of fully automating both hypothesis generation and data analysis for experimental biology. By integrating literature search agents with data analysis agents, Robin can generate hypotheses, propose experiments, interpret experimental results, and generate updated hypotheses, achieving a semi-autonomous approach to scientific discovery. By applying this system, we were able to identify promising therapeutic candidates for dry age-related macular degeneration (dAMD), the major cause of blindness in the developed world [4, 5]. Robin proposed enhancing retinal pigment epithelium phagocytosis as a therapeutic strategy, and identified and confirmed in vitro efficacy for ripasudil and KL001. Ripasudil is a clinically-used Rho kinase (ROCK) inhibitor that has never previously been proposed for treating dAMD. To elucidate the mechanism of ripasudil-induced upregulation of phagocytosis, Robin then proposed and analyzed a follow-up RNA-seq experiment, which revealed upregulation of ABCA1, a lipid efflux pump and possible novel target. All hypotheses, experimental directions, data analyses, and data figures in the main text of this report were produced by Robin. As the first AI system to autonomously discover and validate novel therapeutic candidates within an iterative lab-in-the-loop framework, Robin establishes a new paradigm for AI-driven scientific discovery.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant breakthrough in AI-driven scientific discovery with the development of Robin, a multi-agent system that fully automates hypothesis generation and data analysis in experimental biology. This system has already identified promising therapeutic candidates for dry age-related macular degeneration, a major cause of blindness, demonstrating tangible potential health benefits. The discovery and validation of novel treatments through an autonomous AI system represent a meaningful advancement with broad implications for medical research and patient care.
articles.personality_title: New AI system Robin speeds up discovery of treatments for vision loss
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientific discovery usually involves making observations, creating ideas called hypotheses, testing them with experiments, and analyzing the results. Until now, no computer system could fully handle all these steps on its own.

**What happened** – Researchers have created Robin, a new artificial intelligence (AI) system that can do all parts of scientific discovery by itself. Robin searches scientific papers, comes up with ideas, plans and analyzes experiments, then updates its ideas based on the results. Using Robin, scientists found new possible medicines for dry age-related macular degeneration (dAMD), which is a common cause of blindness in older adults. Robin suggested using two drugs, ripasudil and KL001, to help the eye cells work better. These ideas were tested in the lab and showed positive effects.

**Impact** – Robin is the first AI that can independently find and check new treatments in biology, helping speed up medical research. For example, it found that ripasudil, a drug not previously linked to dAMD, could improve eye cell function by increasing a protein called ABCA1. This discovery might lead to new ways to prevent blindness.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to use Robin to explore other diseases and treatments. They will also continue testing the drugs Robin suggested in more detailed studies to see if they are safe and effective for patients.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The AI system Robin can independently discover and test new medical treatments, showing promise for faster scientific breakthroughs in eye disease and beyond.
articles.personality_title_fr: Robin, un système d'IA qui accélère la découverte de traitements contre la perte de vision
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La découverte scientifique consiste généralement à faire des observations, formuler des hypothèses, réaliser des expériences et analyser les résultats. Jusqu'à présent, aucun système informatique ne pouvait gérer toutes ces étapes de manière autonome.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé Robin, un nouveau système d'intelligence artificielle (IA) capable de réaliser toutes les étapes de la découverte scientifique seul. Robin recherche dans les articles scientifiques, génère des idées, planifie et analyse des expériences, puis met à jour ses hypothèses selon les résultats. Grâce à Robin, des scientifiques ont trouvé de nouveaux médicaments possibles pour la dégénérescence maculaire sèche liée à l'âge (dAMD), une cause fréquente de cécité chez les personnes âgées. Robin a proposé deux médicaments, ripasudil et KL001, pour améliorer le fonctionnement des cellules de l'œil. Ces idées ont été testées en laboratoire avec des résultats positifs.

**Impact** – Robin est la première IA capable de découvrir et valider de nouveaux traitements en biologie de manière autonome, ce qui accélère la recherche médicale. Par exemple, elle a montré que le ripasudil, un médicament jamais associé à la dAMD auparavant, pouvait améliorer la fonction des cellules de l'œil en augmentant une protéine appelée ABCA1. Cette découverte pourrait ouvrir la voie à de nouvelles façons de prévenir la cécité.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d'utiliser Robin pour étudier d'autres maladies et traitements. Ils continueront aussi à tester les médicaments proposés par Robin dans des études plus approfondies pour vérifier leur sécurité et efficacité chez les patients.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le système d'IA Robin peut découvrir et tester de nouveaux traitements médicaux de façon autonome, promettant des avancées plus rapides dans les maladies oculaires et au-delà.
articles.personality_title_es: Robin, un sistema de IA que acelera el descubrimiento de tratamientos para la pérdida de visión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El descubrimiento científico generalmente implica hacer observaciones, crear ideas llamadas hipótesis, realizar experimentos y analizar los resultados. Hasta ahora, ningún sistema informático podía manejar todos estos pasos por sí solo.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon Robin, un nuevo sistema de inteligencia artificial (IA) que puede realizar todas las partes del descubrimiento científico de forma autónoma. Robin busca en artículos científicos, genera ideas, planea y analiza experimentos, y luego actualiza sus hipótesis según los resultados. Usando Robin, los científicos encontraron nuevos posibles medicamentos para la degeneración macular seca relacionada con la edad (dAMD), una causa común de ceguera en adultos mayores. Robin sugirió usar dos medicamentos, ripasudil y KL001, para ayudar a que las células del ojo funcionen mejor. Estas ideas se probaron en el laboratorio y mostraron efectos positivos.

**Impacto** – Robin es la primera IA que puede encontrar y verificar nuevos tratamientos en biología de manera independiente, ayudando a acelerar la investigación médica. Por ejemplo, descubrió que ripasudil, un medicamento no relacionado antes con dAMD, podría mejorar la función celular del ojo aumentando una proteína llamada ABCA1. Este hallazgo podría llevar a nuevas formas de prevenir la ceguera.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar Robin para explorar otras enfermedades y tratamientos. También continuarán probando los medicamentos sugeridos por Robin en estudios más detallados para ver si son seguros y efectivos para los pacientes.

**Frase clave** – El sistema de IA Robin puede descubrir y probar nuevos tratamientos médicos de forma autónoma, mostrando potencial para avances científicos más rápidos en enfermedades oculares y más allá.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of interconnected glowing orbs representing AI agents collaboratively weaving threads of light between an open ancient book of biology and a stylized cellular structure symbolizing the retinal pigment epithelium, with gentle hues of soft gold, teal, and cream highlighting the flow of discovery and innovation.

articles.title: Scientists discover strange link between vitamin D and pain
articles.summary: Low vitamin D levels could be quietly making breast cancer surgery recovery far more painful. In a new study, patients deficient in vitamin D were three times more likely to experience moderate to severe pain after mastectomy surgery and ended up using significantly more opioid medication to cope. Researchers say vitamin D may help regulate how the body processes pain through its effects on inflammation and the immune system.
articles.content:
Women with low vitamin D levels may face a more painful recovery after breast cancer surgery and could require significantly more opioid medication afterward, according to research published online in the journal Regional Anesthesia & Pain Medicine.

The findings suggest that breast cancer patients with vitamin D deficiency (below 30 nmol/L) might benefit from taking vitamin D supplements before undergoing a radical mastectomy.

Researchers say growing evidence points to vitamin D playing an important role in how the body senses and regulates pain. Scientists believe this may be connected to the vitamin's anti inflammatory properties and its effects on the immune system. Vitamin D deficiency is also frequently seen in people with breast cancer.

Study Examined Pain After Breast Cancer Surgery

To explore the connection, researchers carried out a prospective observational study at Fayoum University Hospital in Egypt between September 2024 and April 2025.

The study included 184 women with breast cancer who were preparing to have surgery to remove one breast. Half of the participants had vitamin D deficiency (below 30 nmol/L), while the other half had vitamin D levels above 30 nmol/L. The two groups were otherwise similar, with average ages of 44 and 42.

Doctors and nurses caring for the patients did not know their vitamin D status. All participants received the hospital's standard treatment before, during, and after surgery.

During the operation, patients were given fentanyl to control acute pain. After surgery, everyone received intravenous paracetamol every eight hours. Patients were also able to self administer tramadol, another opioid pain medication, by pressing a control button.

Patients With Low Vitamin D Needed More Opioids

Pain levels were recorded immediately after surgery and again at 6, 12, 18, and 24 hours later. Researchers also tracked nausea, vomiting, sedation levels, and length of hospital stay.

Patients with vitamin D deficiency were three times more likely to experience moderate to severe pain during the first 24 hours after surgery compared with patients who had adequate vitamin D levels.

Researchers noted that none of the patients in either group reported severe pain of 7 or higher on the standard 0 to 10 pain scale. The difference was entirely related to a higher number of patients experiencing moderate pain levels between 4 and 6.

The vitamin D deficient group also required more opioid medication. On average, these patients received 8 μg more fentanyl during surgery, which researchers described as a modest increase.

However, after surgery, the difference became much larger. Patients with low vitamin D used an average of 112mg more tramadol than those with sufficient vitamin D levels. The medication was patient controlled, with doses capped at 50mg per hour.

Vitamin D and Recovery Complications

Opioid medications can lead to side effects such as nausea, vomiting, drowsiness, and confusion. They also carry risks of dependence and addiction.

The study found postoperative nausea occurred more often among patients with vitamin D deficiency. Vomiting was only reported in the deficient group, although researchers said the difference was too small to be considered statistically significant.

The researchers acknowledged several limitations. Because the study was observational and conducted at a single medical center, it cannot prove that low vitamin D directly caused the increase in pain. The team also did not measure inflammatory markers that might explain how vitamin D influences pain. In addition, information about anxiety, depression, cancer stage, previous treatments, and sleep problems before surgery was not collected.

Even with those limitations, the researchers concluded, "Vitamin D deficiency is associated with a higher occurrence of moderate to severe postoperative pain and increased opioid consumption in patients undergoing unilateral modified radical mastectomy."

They added, "Preoperative vitamin D supplementation in breast cancer patients with vitamin D levels below 30 nmol/L may have a role in modulating postoperative pain."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical research finding that links vitamin D deficiency to increased postoperative pain and opioid use in breast cancer surgery patients. This discovery has broad implications for improving pain management and recovery outcomes for many breast cancer patients worldwide. The study suggests a practical intervention—vitamin D supplementation—that could reduce pain and opioid dependency, addressing a critical health issue with potential to improve quality of life and reduce opioid-related risks.
articles.personality_title: Low vitamin D linked to more pain after breast cancer surgery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Breast cancer surgery, such as mastectomy, often causes pain that patients manage with opioid medicines. Opioids can have side effects and risks, so finding ways to reduce pain and opioid use is important. Vitamin D, known for helping bones, might also affect how the body feels pain.

**What happened** – A study in Egypt looked at 184 women having breast removal surgery. Half had low vitamin D levels, and half had normal levels. Doctors did not know who had low vitamin D. After surgery, women with low vitamin D were three times more likely to have moderate pain and needed much more opioid medicine to feel better.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows vitamin D might help control pain after surgery. Women with low vitamin D had more pain and used more opioids, which can cause nausea and other problems. If patients take vitamin D before surgery, they might have less pain and need fewer opioids.

**What's next step** – Researchers suggest more studies to confirm if vitamin D supplements before surgery can lower pain and opioid use. Doctors might start checking vitamin D levels in breast cancer patients and consider supplements as part of treatment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Breast cancer patients with low vitamin D may face more pain after surgery and could benefit from vitamin D supplements to ease recovery.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une faible vitamine D liée à plus de douleur après une chirurgie du cancer du sein
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La chirurgie du cancer du sein, comme la mastectomie, provoque souvent des douleurs que les patientes gèrent avec des opioïdes. Ces médicaments ont des effets secondaires, il est donc important de réduire la douleur et leur utilisation. La vitamine D, connue pour ses bienfaits sur les os, pourrait aussi influencer la perception de la douleur.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une étude en Égypte a suivi 184 femmes subissant une ablation du sein. La moitié avait un faible taux de vitamine D, l’autre moitié un taux normal. Les médecins ne savaient pas qui avait une carence. Après la chirurgie, les femmes avec une faible vitamine D avaient trois fois plus de douleurs modérées et utilisaient beaucoup plus d’opioïdes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la vitamine D pourrait aider à contrôler la douleur après une opération. Les patientes carencées avaient plus mal et prenaient plus d’opioïdes, qui peuvent causer nausées et autres problèmes. Prendre de la vitamine D avant la chirurgie pourrait réduire la douleur et la prise d’opioïdes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs recommandent d’autres études pour vérifier si la supplémentation en vitamine D avant l’opération diminue la douleur et l’usage d’opioïdes. Les médecins pourraient commencer à vérifier les taux de vitamine D chez les patientes et proposer des compléments.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les patientes atteintes d’un cancer du sein avec une faible vitamine D risquent plus de douleurs après la chirurgie et pourraient bénéficier de suppléments pour mieux récupérer.
articles.personality_title_es: Bajos niveles de vitamina D vinculados a más dolor tras cirugía de cáncer de mama
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La cirugía por cáncer de mama, como la mastectomía, suele causar dolor que las pacientes controlan con medicamentos opioides. Estos fármacos pueden tener efectos secundarios, por lo que es importante reducir el dolor y su uso. La vitamina D, conocida por ayudar a los huesos, podría también influir en cómo el cuerpo siente el dolor.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio en Egipto analizó a 184 mujeres que iban a ser operadas para quitar un seno. La mitad tenía bajos niveles de vitamina D y la otra mitad niveles normales. Los médicos no sabían quién tenía deficiencia. Después de la cirugía, las mujeres con poca vitamina D tenían tres veces más probabilidades de sentir dolor moderado y usaron mucho más opioides.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que la vitamina D podría ayudar a controlar el dolor después de la operación. Las pacientes con deficiencia tenían más dolor y usaron más opioides, que pueden causar náuseas y otros problemas. Tomar vitamina D antes de la cirugía podría reducir el dolor y la necesidad de opioides.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren hacer más estudios para confirmar si los suplementos de vitamina D antes de la cirugía pueden disminuir el dolor y el uso de opioides. Los médicos podrían empezar a revisar los niveles de vitamina D en pacientes con cáncer de mama y recomendar suplementos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las pacientes con cáncer de mama y baja vitamina D pueden tener más dolor tras la cirugía y podrían beneficiarse de suplementos para una mejor recuperación.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing golden vitamin D capsule gently illuminating a delicate pink breast-shaped flower bud wrapped in a light, protective mesh of soothing blue threads symbolizing healing and care, set against a warm, muted background of natural earth tones.

articles.title: People who lost the most weight on Ozempic saw huge health benefits
articles.summary: People who lost significant weight while taking Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, or Saxenda had sharply lower risks of major obesity-related health problems, including sleep apnea and kidney disease. Those who gained weight instead faced higher risks — especially for heart failure — even though many patients discontinued the medications within a year.
articles.content:
New research presented at the European Congress on Obesity (ECO 2026) suggests that greater weight loss while taking popular GLP-1 medications such as Ozempic, Wegovy, Saxenda, and Mounjaro may significantly reduce the risk of several obesity-related health problems. The findings also showed that people who gained weight after starting treatment generally faced worse health outcomes.

The study was led by Professor John Wilding from the University of Liverpool in the UK along with fellow researchers.

Greater Weight Loss Linked to Better Health

GLP-1 based medications, including semaglutide (Ozempic, Wegovy), liraglutide (Saxenda), and tirzepatide (Mounjaro), are widely used to treat obesity and type 2 diabetes. Clinical trials have already shown that these drugs can help people lose weight and improve overall health. However, researchers wanted to better understand what happens in real-world settings, where many patients stop taking the medications and weight changes vary considerably.

To explore this, the team analyzed data from Optum Market Clarity, a large US electronic health record and insurance claims database. The researchers examined patients who started GLP-1 based treatments between January 2021 and June 2024.

The study tracked changes in body mass index (BMI) during the first year after treatment began. Researchers then looked at how those weight changes related to later risks of osteoarthritis, chronic kidney disease (CKD), obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), and heart failure through June 2025.

Patients who had already developed one of those conditions before their follow-up weight measurements were excluded from the analysis.

Nearly 90,000 Patients Included

The study involved 89,718 patients overall. Among them, 75.6% started semaglutide medications such as Ozempic or Wegovy, 17.5% started tirzepatide (Mounjaro), and 6.9% began liraglutide (Saxenda).

At the beginning of treatment, the average patient was 57.5 years old with an average BMI of 34.7 kg/m2. About 61% had type 2 diabetes.

Researchers also found that stopping treatment was common. Roughly half of all patients discontinued their GLP-1 medication within one year, defined as going at least 60 days without treatment.

Despite that, the study evaluated all patients according to how much weight they lost or gained during the first year, regardless of whether they stayed on the medication.

Weight Loss Reduced Risk of Major Conditions

During the first year after starting treatment, 27% of patients lowered their BMI by less than 5%, while 22.4% reduced it by between 5% and 10%. Another 14.1% achieved a reduction between 10% and 15%, and 15.8% lowered their BMI by at least 15%. Meanwhile, 20.8% actually gained weight.

Researchers then followed patients for an average of 11 additional months. During that time, the incidence rates per 1,000 person-years were 21.4 for osteoarthritis, 21.1 for chronic kidney disease, 20.3 for obstructive sleep apnea, and 3.9 for heart failure.

Compared with patients who lost less than 5% of their BMI, people who reduced their BMI by at least 15% experienced much lower risks for several conditions. Their risk of osteoarthritis was 37% lower, chronic kidney disease was 30% lower, obstructive sleep apnea was 69% lower, and heart failure was 32% lower. All of the findings were statistically significant except the reduction linked to heart failure.

Weight Gain Was Tied to Worse Outcomes

The study also found troubling trends among patients whose BMI increased after starting treatment.

Compared with people who lost less than 5% of their BMI, those who gained weight had a 10% higher risk of osteoarthritis, a 14% higher risk of chronic kidney disease, a 22% higher risk of obstructive sleep apnea, and a 69% higher risk of heart failure. The increases in sleep apnea and heart failure risk were statistically significant.

The researchers concluded: "In this real-world study where half of patients discontinued GLP-1-based treatment within a year after initiation, not losing weight was associated with worse clinical outcomes while larger reductions were associated with decreased risks. These findings highlight the potential clinical importance of achieving and maintaining weight loss after GLP-1-based treatment initiation."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large-scale, real-world study showing that significant weight loss from GLP-1 medications like Ozempic and Wegovy leads to substantially lower risks of major obesity-related health conditions, including sleep apnea and kidney disease. This has broad positive implications for public health given the prevalence of obesity and related diseases. The study is detailed, involves a large patient cohort, and highlights meaningful health benefits, making it genuinely inspiring and impactful.
articles.personality_title: Significant weight loss with GLP-1 medicines lowers health risks, study finds
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people use medications like Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro, and Saxenda to help lose weight and manage type 2 diabetes. These drugs, called GLP-1 medications, have been shown in clinical trials to support weight loss, but less was known about their effects in everyday life.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Professor John Wilding studied almost 90,000 patients in the US who started GLP-1 treatment between 2021 and 2024. They looked at how much weight people lost or gained during the first year and then checked for health problems like sleep apnea, kidney disease, osteoarthritis, and heart failure over the next year.

**Impact** – The study found that people who lost a lot of weight (at least 15% of their body mass index) had much lower risks of serious health problems: 37% less osteoarthritis, 30% less kidney disease, 69% less sleep apnea, and 32% less heart failure compared to those who lost less than 5%. On the other hand, people who gained weight faced higher risks, especially a 69% increase in heart failure risk. This shows that losing weight with these medicines can lead to important health benefits.

**What's next step** – The findings suggest that helping patients stay on GLP-1 treatments and maintain weight loss could improve health outcomes. Doctors and patients might focus more on long-term support to keep the weight off and reduce risks of obesity-related diseases.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Losing significant weight with GLP-1 medicines like Ozempic is linked to much lower risks of serious health problems, while gaining weight raises those risks.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une perte de poids importante avec les médicaments GLP-1 réduit les risques pour la santé, selon une étude
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes utilisent des médicaments comme Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro et Saxenda pour perdre du poids et gérer le diabète de type 2. Ces médicaments, appelés GLP-1, ont montré dans des essais cliniques qu’ils aident à perdre du poids, mais on savait moins ce qu’ils apportent dans la vie quotidienne.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs dirigés par le professeur John Wilding ont étudié près de 90 000 patients aux États-Unis qui ont commencé un traitement GLP-1 entre 2021 et 2024. Ils ont observé la perte ou la prise de poids pendant la première année, puis surveillé des problèmes de santé comme l’apnée du sommeil, les maladies rénales, l’arthrose et l’insuffisance cardiaque pendant l’année suivante.

**Impact** – L’étude a montré que les personnes qui ont perdu beaucoup de poids (au moins 15 % de leur indice de masse corporelle) avaient des risques beaucoup plus faibles de problèmes graves : 37 % d’arthrose en moins, 30 % de maladies rénales en moins, 69 % d’apnée du sommeil en moins, et 32 % d’insuffisance cardiaque en moins, comparé à ceux qui ont perdu moins de 5 %. À l’inverse, ceux qui ont pris du poids avaient des risques plus élevés, notamment une augmentation de 69 % du risque d’insuffisance cardiaque. Cela prouve que perdre du poids avec ces médicaments peut améliorer la santé.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces résultats suggèrent qu’aider les patients à continuer leur traitement GLP-1 et à maintenir leur perte de poids pourrait améliorer leur santé. Les médecins et les patients pourraient se concentrer davantage sur un soutien à long terme pour garder le poids perdu et réduire les maladies liées à l’obésité.

**En une phrase** – Perdre beaucoup de poids grâce aux médicaments GLP-1 comme Ozempic est lié à des risques bien moindres de graves problèmes de santé, tandis que prendre du poids augmente ces risques.
articles.personality_title_es: Pérdida significativa de peso con medicamentos GLP-1 reduce riesgos de salud, revela estudio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas usan medicamentos como Ozempic, Wegovy, Mounjaro y Saxenda para perder peso y controlar la diabetes tipo 2. Estos medicamentos, llamados GLP-1, han demostrado en ensayos clínicos que ayudan a bajar de peso, pero se sabía menos sobre sus efectos en la vida diaria.

Qué pasó – Investigadores liderados por el profesor John Wilding estudiaron a casi 90,000 pacientes en Estados Unidos que comenzaron tratamiento con GLP-1 entre 2021 y 2024. Observaron cuánto peso perdieron o ganaron en el primer año y luego revisaron problemas de salud como apnea del sueño, enfermedades renales, osteoartritis y insuficiencia cardíaca durante el siguiente año.

Impacto – El estudio encontró que quienes perdieron mucho peso (al menos 15 % de su índice de masa corporal) tuvieron riesgos mucho menores de problemas graves: 37 % menos de osteoartritis, 30 % menos de enfermedad renal, 69 % menos de apnea del sueño y 32 % menos de insuficiencia cardíaca, en comparación con quienes perdieron menos del 5 %. En cambio, quienes ganaron peso enfrentaron riesgos más altos, especialmente un aumento del 69 % en insuficiencia cardíaca. Esto muestra que perder peso con estos medicamentos puede mejorar la salud.

Próximo paso – Los resultados sugieren que ayudar a los pacientes a continuar con el tratamiento GLP-1 y mantener la pérdida de peso podría mejorar los resultados de salud. Los médicos y pacientes podrían enfocarse más en apoyo a largo plazo para conservar el peso perdido y reducir enfermedades relacionadas con la obesidad.

Conclusión en una frase – Perder peso significativo con medicamentos GLP-1 como Ozempic está vinculado a riesgos mucho menores de problemas graves de salud, mientras que aumentar de peso eleva esos riesgos.
